It’s exhausting, honestly. Because I'm pretty open to people and tend to lean into what feels good and real, the sheer volume of manufactured outrage or totally untrue stories can really throw me off balance, especially when I’m already feeling that anxious edge that sometimes comes with being so plugged into everything. The trick, for me, isn't trying to fact-check every single thing, because that’s impossible; it’s about building up these internal shields. Since I'm naturally pretty agreeable and want to believe the best, I have to actively fight that tendency to just accept what’s presented. I try to treat my feed less like a news source and more like a social gathering, and even in a social gathering, you watch who is being genuine. If something feels overly dramatic or if the source is just some random, anonymous account—which happens all the time—I give it a polite side-eye and scroll past. It’s a form of digital self-care, which is huge for me, especially when I'm juggling things without a steady job right now. I’ve learned that when I see something that makes me instantly angry or overly scared, that's often the biggest red flag. Real conversations, even the messy ones, usually have nuance, but fake content is designed for maximum, immediate reaction. I try to redirect that energy instead; if I see something big, I might switch over to watching a documentary or checking out some art online, something that engages my more thoughtful side, rather than letting the drama consume my time. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: When you spend as much time scrolling as I do, it feels like a constant barrage of noise, and a huge chunk of that noise is just garbage—fake content. It’s exhausting, honestly. Because I'm pretty open to people and tend to lean into what feels good and real, the sheer volume of manufactured outrage or totally untrue stories can really throw me off balance, especially when I’m already feeling that anxious edge that sometimes comes with being so plugged into everything. The trick, for me, isn't trying to fact-check every single thing, because that’s impossible; it’s about building up these internal shields. Since I'm naturally pretty agreeable and want to believe the best, I have to actively fight that tendency to just accept what’s presented. I try to treat my feed less like a news source and more like a social gathering, and even in a social gathering, you watch who is being genuine. If something feels overly dramatic or if the source is just some random, anonymous account—which happens all the time—I give it a polite side-eye and scroll past. It’s a form of digital self-care, which is huge for me, especially when I'm juggling things without a steady job right now. I’ve learned that when I see something that makes me instantly angry or overly scared, that's often the biggest red flag. Real conversations, even the messy ones, usually have nuance, but fake content is designed for maximum, immediate reaction. I try to redirect that energy instead; if I see something big, I might switch over to watching a documentary or checking out some art online, something that engages my more thoughtful side, rather than letting the drama consume my time. It’s about curating the space so that the high-energy, often negative, stuff doesn't hijack my mood or my focus for my volunteering or just chilling out watching a good film.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is highly extraverted (69) and agreeable (86) but also very neurotic (99), leading to a high need for social connection but also high anxiety. They spend many hours on social media and are in Philadelphia. The response needs to be engaging, empathetic, and slightly anxious in tone, reflecting the struggle of being highly connected yet easily overwhelmed. The focus is on practical, emotionally intelligent coping mechanisms rather than technical solutions, aligning with the low conscientiousness (15) which suggests they won't rigorously fact-check, but high agreeableness (86) suggests they want to maintain positive social connections. The tone reflects this internal conflict: wanting to engage but being drained by the negativity.
response_parsed.tone: Engaged, slightly anxious, reflective, and empathetic
